role_summary: First field secretary of the Southern Christian Leadership Conference (SCLC); pastor of Mount Canaan Baptist Church in Shreveport, Louisiana (1966-2020); president of the Louisiana Baptist State Convention; general secretary of the National Baptist Convention USA, Inc; SCLC organizer from 1960.
ai_reading: Blake's neighbor counts are moderate (peak 77), with Charles McLaurin (entry 17) as the top match and the broader cluster including James Oscar Jones (entry 57), Nathaniel Hawthorne Jones (entry 92), and Wyatt Tee Walker (entry 132, the SCLC chief of staff). The Walker pairing is the catalog's clearest SCLC-field-secretary-and-headquarters cosine, fitting the cultural placement of Blake as SCLC's first field secretary (1960). The catalog flags a +0.78 reading on the Individual Conscience vs Collective Discipline axis (far toward Collective Discipline) as the most-distinctive structural reading; for a pastor and SCLC field secretary whose entire career was institutional church leadership, the embedding's pull toward Collective Discipline is consistent with the cultural placement, the catalog's most-confirming-alignment reading on a placeholder bio so far.
biographical_paragraph: While Blake's LoC oral history carries a not-auditable audit tier (the catalog cannot independently certify the transcript against primary sources for this entry, and any specific quoted claim drawn from the transcript itself should be confirmed against external archives before citation), his external biographical record is documented in the Louisiana local press at the time of his April 2020 death [src: 1, src: 2]. Blake was born May 20, 1934 in Bossier Parish, Louisiana. He attended Bishop College in Marshall, Texas, where he heard Martin Luther King Jr. speak and joined the Southern Christian Leadership Conference in 1960 as the organization's first field secretary [src: 2]. Following the SCLC's October 1960 annual convention in Shreveport, a gunman attempted to kill him as he drove home, firing bullets through the windshield of his car [src: 2]. In early autumn 1963, after the September 15 Birmingham 16th Street Baptist Church bombing that killed four girls, Blake helped organize a memorial service at Little Union Baptist Church in Shreveport; he was beaten by several Shreveport police officers during the gathering and required stitches for cuts to his scalp [src: 2]. In 1966 he became pastor of Mount Canaan Baptist Church in Shreveport, the post he would hold for the remainder of his ministry. He served as president of the Louisiana Baptist State Convention and as general secretary of the National Baptist Convention USA, Inc. He died April 8, 2020 in Shreveport at eighty-five [src: 2].
movement_context: The SCLC's Shreveport campaign emerged from the local infrastructure that dentist C.O. Simpkins had built through the mid-1950s, when Simpkins (1925-2019) co-founded SCLC with Martin Luther King Jr. and other ministers and invited King to Shreveport to attend to the city's racial conditions [src: 3, src: 4]. Simpkins's home and dental office were firebombed by the Ku Klux Klan in retaliation; he was driven into temporary exile in New York and would not return to Louisiana politics for another quarter century [src: 4]. The 1960 hiring of Harry Blake as SCLC's first field secretary placed a Shreveport-based organizer inside the new SCLC field apparatus at the moment that the SCLC's October annual convention came to Shreveport itself; the shooting attempt on Blake's life after the convention belongs to the same arc of Klan and police-aligned violence that displaced Simpkins [src: 2]. The September 22, 1963 beating at Little Union Baptist Church is the most-documented single incident from the Shreveport organizing campaign of those years. Police Commissioner George D'Artois had denied a march permit for the memorial gathering that Blake helped convene to honor the four girls killed in the Birmingham 16th Street Baptist Church bombing one week earlier; Shreveport police officers rode horses into the sanctuary, pulled Blake from inside by his suit jacket, and beat him outside the church door [src: 2, src: 5]. Witnesses recalled Blake afterward as drenched in blood; medical professionals reportedly hesitated to bring him to local hospitals out of concern for his safety in custody [src: 5]. The Shreveport campaign sat at the western anchor of a broader Louisiana civil rights organizing arc that ran through New Orleans (where Rudy Lombard and the CORE chapter at Southern University at New Orleans had staged the September 1960 Canal Street sit-ins that produced the Supreme Court case Lombard v. Louisiana) and through Bogalusa in Washington Parish (where Fletcher Anderson, in the catalog as entry 25 with Cynthia Baker Anderson, was a founding member of the 1965 Deacons for Defense and Justice chapter that protected NAACP demonstrators against Klan violence at the Crown-Zellerbach paper mill) [src: 6, src: 7]. The Louisiana arc is the subject of Adam Fairclough's monograph Race and Democracy: The Civil Rights Struggle in Louisiana, 1915-1972 (University of Georgia Press, 1995), which centers the NAACP rather than the youth-led organizations of the mid-1960s and traces the persistence of local protest infrastructure across decades [src: 8].
legacy_and_reception: Blake's pastorate at Mount Canaan Baptist Church in Shreveport ran from 1966 until shortly before his death and shaped the church into a community-development institution beyond its pulpit role; he led the construction of Canaan Village affordable housing and the Harry Blake Life Center, both bearing his name in Shreveport [src: 5]. He served as president of the Louisiana Baptist State Convention and as general secretary of the National Baptist Convention USA, Inc., positions that made him one of the senior figures in the Louisiana Black Baptist denominational leadership through the 1990s and 2000s [src: 2]. Blake died April 8, 2020 in Shreveport at the age of eighty-five, his death attributed by family and local press accounts to COVID-19 during the first wave of the pandemic in Louisiana [src: 1, src: 5]. In 2022 the City of Shreveport issued a formal apology for the 1963 police beating at Little Union Baptist Church, more than fifty-eight years after the attack and two years after Blake's death; the apology was reported in conjunction with the seventieth-anniversary retrospective coverage by KTBS [src: 5]. His oral history with the Library of Congress, recorded by David P. Cline in Shreveport on October 3, 2013, is part of the Civil Rights History Project collection (AFC 2010/039) at the American Folklife Center [src: 1].

sources.title: Harry Blake oral history interview conducted by David P. Cline in Shreveport, Louisiana, 2013 October 03
